name.text: Application of vectors in real life

questiontext.text:
A ferry travels due East across a wide river at a constant speed of {@v_ship@} km/h. At the same time, the river current is flowing due North at a constant speed of {@v_current@} km/h.

The ferry continues this journey for {@time_h@} hours.

[[jsxgraph width="400px" height="400px"]] var board = JXG.JSXGraph.initBoard(divid, { boundingbox: [-2, 12, 12, -2], axis: true, showCopyright: false }); var v_s = {#v_ship#} / 3; // Scaling for visibility var v_c = {#v_current#} / 3; // Ship Velocity Vector var p1 = board.create('point', [0, 0], {visible: false}); var p2 = board.create('point', [v_s, 0], {name: 'Ship Velocity', color: 'blue'}); board.create('arrow', [p1, p2], {strokeColor: 'blue', strokeWidth: 3}); // Current Velocity Vector var p3 = board.create('point', [v_s, v_c], {name: 'Current', color: 'green'}); board.create('arrow', [p2, p3], {strokeColor: 'green', strokeWidth: 3}); // Resultant Velocity Vector board.create('arrow', [p1, p3], {strokeColor: 'red', strokeWidth: 2, dash: 2}); board.create('text', [v_s/2, v_c+0.5, "Resultant Path"], {color: 'red'}); [[/jsxgraph]]

Find the :

  1. Magnitude of the resultant velocity of the ferry. [[input:ans1]] km/h [[validation:ans1]][[feedback:prt1]]
  2. Total displacement of the ferry from its starting point. [[input:ans2]] km [[validation:ans2]][[feedback:prt2]]

(Give your answers to 1 decimal place.)

generalfeedback.text:
Worked Solution

To solve this, we treat the velocities as perpendicular components of a vector.

(a) Resultant Velocity

Using Pythagoras' Theorem:

\(\begin{align} v_R &= \sqrt{v_{\text{ship}}^2 + v_{\text{current}}^2} \\ &= \sqrt{{@v_ship@}^2 + {@v_current@}^2} \\ &= {@ta_velocity@} \,\text{ km/h} \end{align}\)

(b) Total Displacement

Displacement can be found by multiplying the resultant velocity by time, or by finding the components of distance first:

\(\begin{align} \text{Distance East} &= {@v_ship@} \times {@time_h@} = {@dist_east@} \text{ km} \\ \text{Distance North} &= {@v_current@} \times {@time_h@} = {@dist_north@} \text{ km} \\\\ s_R &= \sqrt{{@dist_east@}^2 + {@dist_north@}^2} \\ &= {@ta_displacement@} \text{ km} \end{align}\)

prtcorrect.text: Correct answer, well done.

prtpartiallycorrect.text: Your answer is partially correct.

prtincorrect.text: Incorrect answer.
